diagrams.title: Bayes' Theorem Visualization
diagrams.description: A Venn diagram representation showing the relationship between hypothesis and evidence in Bayes' theorem
elements: Two overlapping circles: Circle H representing hypothesis space, Circle E representing evidence space
Intersection area H∩E representing P(H∩E)
Circle H labeled 'Hypothesis H' with area showing P(H)
Circle E labeled 'Evidence E' with area showing P(E)
Arrow from P(H) pointing to P(H|E) showing prior to posterior transformation
Arrow from P(E|H) pointing to P(H|E) showing likelihood contribution
Formula P(H|E) = P(E|H) × P(H) / P(E) displayed in center
Shaded region showing conditional probability relationship
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how Bayes' theorem updates prior beliefs (P(H)) to posterior beliefs (P(H|E)) based on evidence, using the visual relationship between hypothesis and evidence spaces

diagrams.title: Bayesian Classification Model Architecture
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing the complete architecture of Bayes classifier from input to class prediction
elements: Input Features vector [x1, x2, x3, ..., xn] shown as rectangular input box
Arrow pointing from input to Feature Extraction block
Feature Extraction block (rounded rectangle) processing raw data
Arrow from Feature Extraction to Prior Probability Calculator
Prior Probability Calculator block showing computation of P(Ci) for each class
Arrow to Likelihood Calculator computing P(X|Ci) for each class
Likelihood Calculator block with Gaussian/Normal distribution curves inside
Posterior Probability Computation block receiving both prior and likelihood
Inside Posterior block: P(Ci|X) = P(X|Ci) × P(Ci) / P(X) formula displayed
P(X) Calculator computing evidence (normalization factor)
Multiple arrows from Posterior block to Decision Maker
Decision Maker block with MAX function symbol
Output: Class label C* shown as final rectangular output
Decision boundary threshold lines shown connecting to output
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the complete flow of Bayesian classification, understanding how each component contributes to computing posterior probabilities and making classification decisions

diagrams.title: Posterior Probability Computation Flow
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flow diagram showing how posterior probability is calculated for each class
elements: Three parallel columns representing Class C1, Class C2, Class C3
Left column: Prior Probability P(Ci) - horizontal bar chart showing class distribution
Middle column: Likelihood P(X|Ci) - bell curves for each class showing feature distribution
Right column: Posterior Probability P(Ci|X) - computed probability bars
Arrows flowing from Prior to Posterior labeled '× P(Ci)'
Arrows flowing from Likelihood to Posterior labeled '× P(X|Ci)'
Bottom section: Evidence P(X) shown as sum of all numerator products
Formula breakdown: P(Ci|X) ∝ P(X|Ci) × P(Ci)
Comparison operators (>) between posterior values
Winner-take-all circle selecting maximum posterior
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the computation flow: how prior probability and likelihood combine through multiplication to produce posterior probability, and how the normalization by P(X) ensures probabilities sum to 1

diagrams.title: Bayes Decision Boundary Illustration
diagrams.description: 2D feature space showing decision boundaries created by Bayes classifier for two classes
elements: X-axis labeled 'Feature 1 (x1)' with scale 0-10
Y-axis labeled 'Feature 2 (x2)' with scale 0-10
Scatter plot showing two classes: Class A (red circles) and Class B (blue triangles)
Overlapping Gaussian ellipses for each class showing probability density
Solid curved line representing Bayes decision boundary
Region on left of boundary labeled 'Classify as A' shaded light red
Region on right of boundary labeled 'Classify as B' shaded light blue
Point Xtest shown as black star with coordinates (4.5, 5.5)
Dashed lines from Xtest to each class centroid
Decision boundary equation displayed: P(A|X) = P(B|X)
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize how Bayes classifier creates optimal decision boundaries by finding points where posterior probabilities of competing classes are equal, minimizing classification error

diagrams.title: Bayes Classifier vs Other Classifiers Comparison
diagrams.description: Comparative architecture diagram showing Bayes classifier position among ML classifiers
elements: Top level: Machine Learning classifiers as main container
Left branch: Generative classifiers (Bayes-based) - highlighted in green
Sub-branch from Generative: Naive Bayes (Gaussian, Multinomial, Bernoulli)
Sub-branch from Generative: Gaussian Discriminant Analysis
Right branch: Discriminative classifiers - highlighted in blue
Discriminative branch showing: Logistic Regression, SVM, Neural Networks
Decision boundary comparison: three side-by-side 2D plots
Plot 1: Smooth curved boundaries (Bayes/GDA)
Plot 2: Linear boundary (Logistic Regression)
Plot 3: Complex non-linear boundary (SVM with kernel)
Performance comparison bars showing accuracy metrics
Legend showing classifier types and their characteristics
diagrams.purpose: Students understand Bayes classifier as a generative model, distinguishing it from discriminative approaches, and learn when each type is preferable based on data characteristics and computational requirements

charts.title: Bayesian Classification Process Flowchart
charts.content: Flowchart showing 6-step process: (1) Input feature vector → (2) Compute prior probabilities P(C) from training data → (3) Compute likelihood P(X|C) using probability distributions → (4) Calculate posterior P(C|X) = P(X|C)×P(C)/P(X) → (5) Compare posterior probabilities across classes → (6) Assign class with highest posterior. Each step shown as rectangular box with arrows connecting sequentially, with decision diamond at step 5 showing 'Is P(Ci|X) > P(Cj|X)?' leading to final class label output.

charts.title: Probability Types in Bayesian Classification
charts.content: Three-column comparison table: Column 1 'Prior Probability P(C)' - definition: initial belief about class distribution before seeing data, source: relative frequency in training data, example: P(Spam) = 0.3 from 30% spam emails; Column 2 'Likelihood P(X|C)' - definition: probability of observing features given class, source: probability distribution fitting (Gaussian, multinomial), example: P(words|spam) for spam detection; Column 3 'Posterior Probability P(C|X)' - definition: updated belief about class after observing features, source: computed via Bayes theorem, example: P(spam|words) = 0.85 final classification probability.

charts.title: Bayes Classifier Properties Comparison
charts.content: Four-row comparison table: Row 1 'Optimality' - Bayes classifier is theoretically optimal (minimum error), others approximate; Row 2 'Training Data Requirement' - Can work with smaller datasets, requires sufficient samples per class; Row 3 'Computational Complexity' - Low at classification time (lookup table), higher during training for distribution fitting; Row 4 'Assumptions' - Independence assumption in Naive Bayes (can be relaxed in full Bayes), other classifiers make different assumptions about decision boundaries.

charts.title: Types of Bayesian Classifiers
charts.content: Hierarchical classification chart: Level 1 'Bayesian Classifiers' splits into (A) Naive Bayes Classifier with subtypes: Gaussian Naive Bayes (continuous features), Multinomial Naive Bayes (text/count data), Bernoulli Naive Bayes (binary features); (B) Full Bayes Classifier requiring probability density estimation; (C) Bayesian Networks representing conditional dependencies. Each subtype shows specific use cases and example applications.

branches: Prior Probability P(C): Initial class distribution, computed from training data, represents belief before evidence
Likelihood P(X|C): Probability of features given class, models feature distributions per class, computed using statistical distributions
Posterior Probability P(C|X): Updated class probability after observing features, computed via Bayes theorem, determines final classification
Decision Rule: Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) selects class with highest posterior, Equivalent to Maximum Likelihood when priors are equal
Bayes Optimal Classifier: Theoretical minimum error classifier, requires knowing true probability distributions, serves as performance benchmark
Naive Bayes Assumption: Features conditionally independent given class, simplifies computation, enables practical implementation
Applications: Spam filtering, text classification, medical diagnosis, sentiment analysis
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all key concepts in Bayesian classification - from prior probabilities through likelihood and posterior computation to classification decisions, showing how Bayes theorem provides a principled framework for probabilistic classification
